questions.clue: This network of secret routes and safe houses, organized primarily by free and enslaved African Americans, helped more than 100,000 enslaved people escape to the Northern United States and Canada before the Civil War.

questions.clue: The assassination of this Austro-Hungarian heir to the throne by Gavrilo Princip in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914 set off the chain of events that ignited the First World War.

questions.clue: On February 20, 1962, this American astronaut circled the Earth three times aboard Friendship 7, becoming the first American to orbit the planet; he later flew again at age 77, becoming the oldest person to reach orbit.

citations.title: John Glenn

questions.clue: Styling himself 'the Sun King,' this French monarch held the throne for 72 years and 110 days — the longest reign of any sovereign in recorded history — and made the Palace of Versailles his royal seat to consolidate absolute power.

questions.clue: This Carthaginian general invaded Italy in 218 BC by leading North African war elephants across the Alps and inflicted a devastating defeat on Rome at the Battle of Cannae in 216 BC.

questions.clue: While imprisoned in 1946, this American civil rights leader and Muslim minister joined the Nation of Islam, replaced his birth surname 'Little' with a symbol of his unknown African ancestral name, and discarded what he called his 'white slavemaster name.'

questions.clue: In his 1961 farewell address, this Republican president coined the phrase 'military–industrial complex' to warn Americans against the growing political influence of the defense industry on government policy.

questions.clue: In 1778, Captain James Cook discovered the Hawaiian archipelago and named it after this British First Lord of the Admiralty, the chief backer of his Pacific voyage.

questions.clue: The birthplace and early headquarters of the Nazi Party in the 1920s, this German city was designated 'Capital of the Movement' and later lent its name to the 1938 agreement in which Britain and France let Hitler annex the Sudetenland.

questions.clue: Historians regard this nearly 900-day engagement between Soviet and Nazi forces as one of the deadliest in military history; over a million civilians died before it was lifted in January 1944.
